Independent Civic Analysis — Not Official City of Cambridge Data. Data from Cambridge Open Data (data.cambridgema.gov) and Cambridge Open Budget portal (budget.data.cambridgema.gov). FY2025 = operating year July 2024 – June 2025. Contract dollar values are not published in Cambridge's open data — only counts and terms. Budget line items represent adopted appropriations, not actual expenditures. Contact: Oversight-MA@pm.me

FY2025 Executive Summary

Cambridge's FY2025 adopted operating budget is $848M across 2,444 budget line items and 35 departments. Overtime is budgeted at $8.1M across 58 lines — Police leads at $4.0M. Debt service totals $102M (principal + interest). 842 contracts are active; 779 have zero renewals remaining and must be competitively rebid. 53 capital projects received $75M in FY2025 appropriations. Contract dollar values are not published in Cambridge's open data.

Data note — contract dollar values: Cambridge's Open Data portal does not publish the dollar value of individual contracts. The city uses MUNIS (Tyler Technologies) as its ERP; contract payment data is not exposed in the public API. As a proxy, the operating budget's Professional & Technical Services line ($64.8M FY2025) captures contracted professional and consulting work. A full picture of contract values requires a public records request (M.G.L. c. 66 §10) to the Purchasing Division.

FY2025 Actual Expenditures — Audited ACFR

Actual vs budgeted spending for FY2025 (year ended June 30, 2025), from the City of Cambridge Annual Comprehensive Financial Report. This is audited data — the closest thing to actual transaction records available in a public document.

Source: City of Cambridge FY2025 ACFR, Schedule of Expenditures – Budgetary Basis, p. 81–86. Audited by an independent auditor, published January 30, 2026 by City Auditor Joseph McCann.
Download FY2025 ACFR PDF (1.4 MB)  ·  This is NOT transaction-level data — it is department/category-level aggregates. For individual payment records, a public records request is required.

Overtime Budget Analysis — FY2025

Overtime is the largest discretionary compensation lever and a perennial oversight concern. FY2025 includes 58 overtime budget lines totaling $8.1M — concentrated in Police, Fire, and Public Works.

Salary Budget — FY2025 Positions

Position-level salary budget. Important: Cambridge Public Schools (~1,900 positions) are absent from this dataset for FY2025 — the source data on Cambridge Open Data is incomplete. FY2025 reflects only non-Education departments.

⚠ FY2025 salary data is missing the Education service (~1,900 CPS positions, ~$225M budgeted wages). The FY2025 position data here covers General Government, Public Safety, Human Services, and Community Maintenance only.

Contracts Active in FY2025

All contracts active during FY2025 (status=active OR term overlapping July 2024 – June 2025). Dollar values are not published in Cambridge's open data.

On contract dollar values: Cambridge's MUNIS procurement system does not expose contract dollar amounts through its open data API. What is available: contract title, vendor, department, start/end dates, renewal options, emergency designation, and procurement classification. To obtain actual contract values, file a public records request with the Cambridge Purchasing Division under M.G.L. c. 66 §10.

Active Fixed-Term Contracts (No Renewal Clause) rebid on expiry

These 779 active contracts have no renewal option defined (has_renewal_option=false). They are not in immediate danger — but when they expire, Cambridge must competitively rebid them under M.G.L. c. 30B rather than simply extending them.

Outstanding Bonds & Long-Term Debt — June 30, 2025

Cambridge carries $690.7M in general obligation bonds, funded by a series of competitive bond sales since 2012. Bonds finance capital projects — schools, roads, sewers, fire stations — and are repaid through annual debt service from the operating budget.

Source: City of Cambridge FY2025 ACFR, Notes to Financial Statements (p. 50–52) and Schedule of Bonds and Notes Payable (p. 98–99). Audited by independent auditor, Jan 30 2026.

Legal debt limit: Massachusetts law caps general obligation debt at 5% of equalized assessed valuation ($85.4B × 5% = $4.27B). Cambridge has used only 24.75% of this limit, leaving $3.21B of additional debt capacity.

Hidden liabilities: The bond debt ($690.7M) is smaller than Cambridge's unfunded other post-employment benefit (OPEB) liability of $749.8M — retiree health insurance obligations that do not require voter approval or appear in the debt limit calculation.

Bond Categories — FY2025 Outstanding

CategoryRatesFinal Maturity Added FY25Retired FY25 OutstandingDue < 1yr

Annual Debt Service Schedule — Principal & Interest

Total future debt service: $875.7M ($690.7M principal + $185.0M interest). Annual payments decline from $108.6M (FY2026) to $49.7M (FY2041-2045) as bonds mature.

PeriodPrincipalInterest Total

Individual Bond Issuances — What Was Financed (click to sort)

Each row is a separate bond issuance. Major capital projects (Tobin School, Fire HQ, King Open School, street reconstruction, sewer reconstruction) are typically funded by multiple issuances over several years.

Project / Purpose Rate Issued Matures FY24 Balance Retired FY25 FY25 Balance

Other Long-Term Obligations (not in debt limit)

These liabilities are NOT subject to the 5% debt limit and do NOT require voter approval. The OPEB liability alone ($749.8M) exceeds the entire bond portfolio.

ObligationBalance June 30, 2025Notes
